front: Poem: this poem opens 'After every war / someone has to clean up'
Poem: in this poem, someone is stretched out 'in the grass that has overgrown / causes and effects'
Poem: Eavan Boland titled her anthology After Every War in homage to this Szymborska poem
Poem: this poem ends 'Every beginning / is only a sequel, after all, / and the book of events / is always open halfway through'
Poem: in this poem, two lovers would be 'amazed to hear that Chance has been toying with them for years,' having shared near-encounters like 'a sorry muttered in a crowd'
Poem: this Szymborska poem inspired the Red entry in the Three Colors film trilogy
Poem: in this poem, a 'semi-moonman' (allegory for Stalin) is told 'we've got Shakespeare there' and 'we play solitaire and violin'
back: "Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition" (Wisława Szymborska)
work: Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition

front: Poem: this poem ends with the speaker 'stomping my feet for warmth on the everlasting snow' and declares 'down there, we've got Wednesday, bread and alphabets'
back: "Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition" (Wisława Szymborska)
work: Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition

front: Poem: this Szymborska collection (1957), her third volume, contains 'Notes from a Nonexistent Himalayan Expedition' and 'Brueghel's Two Monkeys'
Poem: in 'Brueghel's Two Monkeys,' this poet takes 'an exam on the history of mankind' inspired by a Pieter Bruegel painting of two chained monkeys
Poem: this Szymborska poem about 9/11 describes people 'halted in life' and kept 'above the earth toward the earth'; the poet declines to 'add a last line'
Poem: this Szymborska poem's speaker says 'look down on yourselves from the stars' and imagines 'it turns out I was right / But nothing has come of it'
Poem: this Szymborska poem opens 'die — you can't do that to a cat / Since what can a cat do / in an empty apartment?'
Poem: this Szymborska poem concludes 'one hundred out of one hundred' are mortal, comparing people 'wise in hindsight' vs. 'wise in foresight'
Poem: this Szymborska poem begins 'Why does this written doe bound through these written woods?' and describes the title feeling as 'Revenge of a mortal hand'
Poem: in this Szymborska poem, death 'can't take a joke / find a star, make a bridge'
Poem: in this Szymborska poem, Darwin 'read novels to relax / But only certain kinds: / nothing that ended unhappily'
Author: this poet's work is characterized by 'ironic precision'; her most frequent English translator is Clare Cavanagh
Author: this Polish poet was popularized abroad by her German translator Karl Dedecius
Author: this poet published pseudonymously in the magazine Kultura
Author: in her Nobel speech, this poet argued that admitting 'I don't know' propels writers whose 'self-dissatisfaction' is 'clipped together with a giant paperclip by literary historians'
Poem: this 1995 Szymborska collection's title poem states that 'the view doesn't view itself' and an object 'does just fine, without a name'
back: View with a Grain of Sand (Wisława Szymborska)

front: Poem: this Szymborska poem about Lot's wife says she 'flew upward / until darkness fell from the heavens / and with it scorching gravel'
